original_abstract:
This article provides a detailed exegetical and structural analysis of Sirach 6:18-22, identifying it as the foundational movement of a larger poetic unit (Sir 6:18-37) dedicated to the acquisition of wisdom. The author argues that this section functions as a pedagogical "preamble," establishing the fundamental prerequisite for achieving wisdom: the voluntary submission to a rigorous and lifelong process of formation (*paideia*).

Structurally, the study highlights two contrasting sub-sections. In the first (vv. 18-19), the sage employs agricultural metaphors—plowing and sowing—to frame the search for wisdom as a disciplined labor that begins in youth and extends into old age. Despite the emphasis on long-term effort and patience, the author notes an underlying optimistic tone, as the text promises that the rewards of such toil are accessible and "near."

The second sub-section (vv. 20-22) serves as a warning, describing those who lack the inner disposition to endure the "weight" of wisdom. Utilizing the imagery of a "testing stone," Sirach characterizes the uneducated and the "heartless" (those devoid of intellectual and moral resolve) as being fundamentally incompatible with wisdom’s demands. These individuals lack the necessary persistence and inevitably reject the burden of formation.

Ultimately, the analysis explores the linguistic and etymological nuances of the term "wisdom" in the Greek and Hebrew traditions, concluding that while wisdom is theoretically available to all, it remains functionally elitist. Its "exclusivity" is not rooted in social status or origin, but in the rarity of the human will to embrace the discipline and patience required to obtain it. In this way, Sirach reaffirms that while wisdom is a divine gift, its manifestation in the believer's life is inextricably linked to human effort.
humanized_abstract:
This paper provides an exegetical and structural analysis of Sir 6:18–22 with a view to demonstrating that the central block of the poem on wisdom as a gift in the book of Sirach is an introduction to the greater wisdom school of thought that makes up the poem in Sir 6:18–37. The core of the poem in Sir 6:18–22 can be seen as the opening preamble to the paideia (school of education) of the wisdom school of thought. It deals with the foundational issue of the choice that has to be made at the outset of the process of formation if one wants to acquire wisdom.

In this section of the study, the sages of the book of Sirach divide the section into two different sub-sections. In the first sub-section (vv. 18-19), the sages use imagery that is agricultural in nature, describing the work of plowing and sowing the soil, in order to illustrate the work of seeking wisdom from an early age until later years. The sub-section points out that the work of seeking wisdom is a work that demands patience, yet at the same time it presents the seeker with an optimistic picture, since it promises him that the fruit of his labour is attainable and “near”.

The second subsection (vv. 20-22) forms a warning. It deals with those who do not have the interior disposition to be able to bear the weight of wisdom. The ‘testing stone’ (Sir 20) is an image that describes the uneducated and the heartless as being unworthy of wisdom. In fact, they do not have the necessary endurance to undergo the process of formation.

We saw that using the full range of linguistic and etymological resources of ancient Greek and Hebrew, wisdom, as defined by its parameters, is something that is in principle available to everyone; and yet it appears to be functionally elitist. It is not that wisdom is the preserve of one social class or group more than others. Its exclusivity is a different kind of matter. People's willingness and ability to commit themselves to the demands of acquiring wisdom is different from one individual to the next. So for Siracides (as in Judaism generally) wisdom is a divine don; yet in the believer there is an element of human initiative and struggle.
